pattern: An element can only ever fall to its smallest prime factor in a single operation, so making the array non-decreasing is a right-to-left greedy chase of the tightest ceiling each element can still duck under.
hints: Work out what one operation really does to a number: dividing it by its own greatest proper divisor always leaves exactly that number's smallest prime factor, and applying the operation again to a prime does nothing (it stays put).

Because operations only ever decrease a value and the last element can never be changed to accommodate earlier ones, sweep from right to left, keeping track of the largest value the current position is still allowed to be.

At each position, if the number already fits under the current ceiling, keep it unchanged and lower the ceiling to that value; otherwise the only move available is replacing it with its smallest prime factor -- take that if it fits under the ceiling, or declare the array unfixable if even that is still too large.

edge_inputs.description: statement example, impossible because 7 is prime and cannot shrink below itself
statement example, already non-decreasing needs zero operations
descending composite chain needing repeated reductions
two elements at the value extremes
a prime blocks the only possible reduction
already-sorted ascending primes need no operations
a power of two needs only one reduction to its smallest prime factor

stress.description: random values biased toward smaller magnitudes so composite reductions actually get exercised
stress.generator_python:
def gen(rng, size):
    n = max(1, min(size, 100000))
    nums = [rng.randint(1, 2000) for _ in range(n)]
    return [nums]
